Corporate is tilting at resume windmills

[Sitemap](https://medium.com/sitemap/sitemap.xml)

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:640/format:webp/1*tiZByUVxEjx-WJjPzpQQbg.png)

[Ryan Carson](https://x.com/ryancarson/) recently posted a list of traits for the “super-unicorn” employee

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:640/format:webp/1*Fur9gyT05KhfnNyUWA5L7Q.jpeg)

He’s right. That’s exactly who you want.

Here’s what he didn’t say: nothing in modern hiring is in now way set up to consistently procure that kind of candidate (or even one in that spirit).

The Gap
-------

We’ve built an entire infrastructure around hiring. Applicant tracking systems. Structured interviews. Take-home assignments. Reference checks. LinkedIn Recruiter seats that cost more than some employees.

All of it optimizes for one thing: the resume.

The resume tells you where someone worked. What title they held. How long they stayed. It tells you nothing about how they think, how they behave under pressure, or how they relate to the people around them.

We aren’t hiring for competence. We’re hiring for the appearance of competence.

And appearance can be faked.

The Fraud Problem
-----------------

I’ve now had three separate conversations — with founders, with security professionals, with people running technical screens — where the same thing came up: fake candidates.

Not exaggerated resumes. Not inflated titles. Actual fraudulent actors, including state-sponsored operatives, applying to technical roles at startups.

In one case, the security review was so severe it triggered a complete overhaul of the company’s hiring protocols. The candidate had passed the resume screen. They’d passed the initial interviews. They looked perfect on paper.

Because paper is easy to fake.

This isn’t a rare edge case. It’s a systemic vulnerability. When your entire filtering system relies on self-reported credentials — credentials that can be fabricated, purchased, or stolen — you’ve built a castle on sand.

The Coasting Problem
--------------------

Fraud is dramatic. But there’s a quieter failure mode that’s far more common.

I recently spoke with a founder who wanted to hire someone from a prestigious company. Decade of tenure. Senior title. All the right logos on the resume.

Several rounds of interviews in, it became clear: this person had been coasting. They couldn’t articulate what they’d actually built. They had no ownership over outcomes. They’d simply… existed in the building while the company’s reputation did the heavy lifting. They dodged that bullet — but wasted a ton of time in the process.

The brand name meant nothing. The tenure meant nothing. The only signal that would have revealed the truth was asking the right questions — and having something to check the answers against.

==> /home/workspace/Knowledge/content-library/articles/vrijen/moneyball-for-hiring.md <==
Moneyball….for Hiring?
==> /home/workspace/Knowledge/content-library/articles/vrijen/talent-agent-economy.md <==
The Talent Agent Economy

[Sitemap](https://medium.com/sitemap/sitemap.xml)

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:640/format:webp/1*D_BE23N5FcAQejq7qSPQwQ.png)

As companies shrink, the impact of each remaining employee grows.

In the old paradigm, a company of 5,000 could absorb 500 coasters. In the new paradigm, a company of 50 cannot absorb five. When your team is small, every hire is a “Superunicorn” requirement.

But as we’ve seen, the current infrastructure — the resume, the ATS, the credential — is architecturally incapable of finding them. It only finds the people who are good at *appearing* competent.

So, how do we find the people who actually *are*?

The answer isn’t a better job board. It’s a shift in how talent is represented and verified. It’s the move toward the **Talent Agent Economy.**

Representation for the Elite
----------------------------

In sports and entertainment, the best talent doesn’t “apply” for roles. They have representation.

An agent doesn’t just negotiate the contract; they manage the **scouting report.** They curate the “tape” we talked about in Part 2. They verify the mental model of the person they represent.

In tech, we’re seeing the emergence of this same model. As the “Superunicorn” becomes the single most valuable asset in a company’s arsenal, those individuals will stop being treated as labor commodities and start being treated as elite assets.

They won’t be on LinkedIn. They’ll be managed.

The Mental Model as Verified Asset
----------------------------------

The breakthrough happens when we stop relying on the **Credentialing Mirage (see my *Don Hirexote* article)**  and start relying on **Longitudinal Correlated Data.**

This is what we are building at Careerspan (www.mycareerspan.com).

Imagine a system that doesn’t just store a resume, but maps a person’s mental model over one’s “career span.” It tracks how they think, how they solve problems, and how they relate to others — not in a single interview, but as a persistent record of behavior and reflection.

When a founder looks at a candidate through this lens, they aren’t just reading marketing material. They are looking at correlated data points:

* Does their self-reflection on a project match the feedback from their peers?
* Does their described “scrappiness” show up in the actual timeline of their builds?
* Does the “tape” confirm that they led the team through a crisis, or were they just in the room?

This is how you solve the fraud problem. You can fake a resume. You can even fake an interview. But it is nearly impossible to fake a consistent mental model across years of correlated data.

Scaling the Non-Obvious
-----------------------

The “Talent Agent” of the future won’t just be a human in a suit. It will be an infrastructure of verification.

By mapping these mental models, we can move from **passive filtering** to **active matching at scale.** We can find the “non-obvious” Superunicorn — the engineer in a mid-sized firm who has the agency of a founder — because the data shows us their “tape” long before their resume does.
